title: React Intro
content:
React Introduction

The UI Library

React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces, originally developed by Facebook. It completely revolutionized frontend development by allowing you to build fast, interactive single-page applications (SPAs) using reusable UI components.



React's secret weapon is the Virtual DOM. Instead of updating the entire webpage when data changes, React calculates the exact differences in memory and efficiently updates only the parts of the page that changed.

title: Getting Started
content:
Getting Started

Setting up the Environment

To run React locally, you need to install Node.js and npm (Node Package Manager). Modern developers typically use build tools like Vite or Next.js to spin up a new React project in seconds.

Terminal Commands
# Open your terminal and run this to create a new Vite project:
npm create vite@latest my-react-app -- --template react

# Navigate into the folder:
cd my-react-app

# Install dependencies and start the local server:
npm install
npm run dev

title: JSX
content:
React JSX

JavaScript + XML

JSX stands for JavaScript XML. It allows you to write HTML-like code directly inside your JavaScript files. Before running in the browser, tools like Babel compile this JSX down into regular JavaScript objects.

Writing JSX
// This looks like HTML, but it's actually JavaScript!
const element = <h1 className="title">Hello React!</h1>;

// You can inject JavaScript variables directly into JSX using curly braces {}
const name = "Sanjith";
const greeting = <p>Welcome back, {name}!</p>;

title: Components
content:
React Components

The Building Blocks

Components are the core of React. They allow you to split your UI into independent, reusable pieces. Whether you are building a website or a mobile application like MealMitra, the entire interface is just a tree of nested components.



Functional Component
// A component is just a JavaScript function that returns JSX
// Component names MUST start with a Capital letter!
function Hello() {
  return (
    <div>
      <h1>Hello World!</h1>
      <p>This is my first component.</p>
    </div>
  );
}

// You use it like a custom HTML tag: <Hello />

title: Props
content:
React Props

Passing Data

Props (short for properties) are how we pass data from a parent component down to a child component. They make components dynamic and highly reusable.

Using Props
// The child component receives 'props' as an object argument
function Greeting(props) {
  return <h1>Hello, {props.name}!</h1>;
}

function App() {
  return (
    <div>
      {/* Passing the 'name' prop from the parent */}
      <Greeting name="Sanjith" />
      <Greeting name="Developer" />
    </div>
  );
}

title: State (useState)
content:
React State and useState

Component Memory

State is used to manage data that changes over time inside a specific component (like the current meal plan selected by a user). In modern functional React, we use the useState Hook to add state. When state updates, React automatically re-renders the component!



The useState Hook
import { useState } from 'react';

function Counter() {
  // count is the current value. setCount is the function to update it.
  // 0 is the initial starting value.
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  return (
    <div>
      <p>You clicked {count} times</p>
      <button onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}>
        Add +1
      </button>
    </div>
  );
}

title: Lists & Keys
content:
Lists and Keys

Rendering Arrays

React lets you display lists of data using the standard JavaScript map() function. Whenever you render a list, you must assign a unique key prop to each item so React can efficiently track which items have changed, been added, or been removed.

Mapping Data
function GroceryList() {
  const items = [
    { id: 1, name: 'Apples' },
    { id: 2, name: 'Rice' },
    { id: 3, name: 'Chicken' }
  ];

  return (
    <ul>
      {items.map(item => (
        // The key MUST be unique and placed on the outermost element of the loop
        <li key={item.id}>{item.name}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}

title: Events
content:
React Events

Handling Interactions

React lets you handle standard DOM events like clicks, input changes, and form submissions. Event handlers in React are written in camelCase (like onClick instead of onclick), and you pass a function rather than a string.

Event Handlers
function ActionButton() {
  
  // Define the event handler function
  const handleClick = () => {
    alert('Button was clicked!');
  };

  return (
    // Pass the function to the onClick attribute (do not add () after the function name!)
    <button onClick={handleClick}>
      Click Me
    </button>
  );
}

title: Effects (useEffect)
content:
useEffect Hook

Side Effects & Lifecycle

The useEffect Hook lets you synchronize a component with an external system. You use it to fetch data from an API, manually change the DOM, or set up subscriptions when the component first loads onto the screen.



Fetching Data
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

function DataLoader() {
  const [data, setData] = useState(null);

  // This effect runs ONCE when the component first mounts (because of the empty [] array)
  useEffect(() => {
    console.log('Component rendered! Fetching data...');
    // Simulated API call
    setTimeout(() => setData("API Data Loaded"), 2000);
  }, []); // The Dependency Array

  return <p>{data ? data : "Loading..."}</p>;
}

title: Conditional UI
content:
Conditional Rendering

Dynamic Interfaces

In React, you can show or hide elements based on conditions using standard JavaScript logic, such as `if` statements, the logical `&&` operator, or the ternary `? :` operator directly inside your JSX.

Ternary Logic
function App({ isLoggedIn }) {
  return (
    <div>
      {/* If isLoggedIn is true, render Dashboard. Otherwise, render Login. */}
      {isLoggedIn ? <Dashboard /> : <Login />}
      
      {/* Using logical &&: This only renders if there are no errors */}
      {!error && <p>Everything is running smoothly.</p>}
    </div>
  );
}

title: Forms
content:
React Forms

Controlled Components

In traditional HTML, form inputs handle their own state. In React, we use "Controlled Components"—meaning React State is the single source of truth. We bind the input's value to a state variable, and use onChange to update it.

Controlled Input
import { useState } from 'react';

function NameForm() {
  const [inputValue, setInputValue] = useState("");

  return (
    <form>
      <label>Name: </label>
      <input 
        type="text" 
        value={inputValue} 
        onChange={(e) => setInputValue(e.target.value)} 
      />
      <p>You are typing: {inputValue}</p>
    </form>
  );
}

title: React Summary
content:
React Summary 🚀

Foundation Built! 🏆

You have successfully conquered the foundational building blocks of React! Because React Native uses this exact same architecture under the hood, you are now perfectly positioned to build robust mobile applications.

  • Architecture: Building reusable UIs using JSX, Components, and Props.
  • Interactivity: Managing component memory using the useState hook.
  • Data Rendering: Rendering arrays with Lists and Keys, and handling Events.
  • Lifecycles: Connecting to APIs and handling side effects using useEffect.

These are the exact tools used by frontend engineers every single day. Let's keep building!

title: React Router
content:
React Router

Navigating SPAs

React Router is the standard library for handling navigation in React web applications. It allows you to create multiple pages, handle URLs, and manage browser history without reloading the page.



Router Setup
import { BrowserRouter, Routes, Route } from 'react-router-dom';

// Elements passed to the Route must be in JSX format
function App() {
  return (
    <BrowserRouter>
      <Routes>
        <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
        <Route path="/about" element={<About />} />
      </Routes>
    </BrowserRouter>
  );
}

Note: For mobile apps, you will use React Navigation instead of React Router, but the core routing concepts are very similar!

title: Context API
content:
useContext Hook

Global State

The useContext Hook lets you share state globally between components without passing props through dozens of nested layers (a frustrating problem known as "prop drilling"). This is perfect for sharing a user's login session or theme preferences across an entire meal planning application!



Providing Context
import React, { useContext } from 'react';

// 1. Create the Context
const UserContext = React.createContext();

function App() {
  return (
    // 2. Provide the data to all children components
    <UserContext.Provider value="Sanjith">
      <Dashboard />
    </UserContext.Provider>
  );
}

function Dashboard() {
  // 3. Consume the data anywhere deep in the component tree!
  const user = useContext(UserContext);
  return <h2>Welcome to your dashboard, {user}</h2>;
}

title: useEffect
content:
useEffect Hook

Managing Side Effects

We touched on this earlier, but it is crucial. The useEffect Hook lets you perform side effects—like fetching data from a backend database, subscribing to events, or running timers—after the component renders.

Dependency Array
import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';

function RecipeViewer({ recipeId }) {
  useEffect(() => {
    console.log('Fetching data for recipe: ' + recipeId);
    
    // The return function acts as a "cleanup" when the component unmounts
    return () => console.log('Cleaning up listeners...');
    
  // The dependency array: This effect ONLY runs when 'recipeId' changes!
  }, [recipeId]); 
  
  return <p>Viewing Recipe {recipeId}</p>;
}

title: useRef
content:
React useRef Hook

Persisting Values

The useRef Hook lets you persist values across renders without triggering a re-render (unlike useState). It is most commonly used to access DOM elements directly, such as forcing an input field to focus when a page loads.



Direct DOM Access
import { useRef } from 'react';

function TextInputWithFocusButton() {
  const inputEl = useRef(null);

  const onButtonClick = () => {
    // Directly access the DOM node to trigger focus
    inputEl.current.focus();
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <input ref={inputEl} type="text" placeholder="Type here..." />
      <button onClick={onButtonClick}>Focus the input</button>
    </div>
  );
}

title: useReducer
content:
React useReducer Hook

Complex State Logic

The useReducer Hook is an alternative to useState for managing complex state logic that involves multiple sub-values, or when the next state depends on the previous one. It functions very similarly to Redux reducers.

Reducer Example
import { useReducer } from 'react';

// The reducer function handles all state update logic based on "actions"
function reducer(state, action) {
  switch (action.type) {
    case 'increment': return { count: state.count + 1 };
    case 'decrement': return { count: state.count - 1 };
    default: throw new Error();
  }
}

function Counter() {
  const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, { count: 0 });

  return (
    <div>
      Count: {state.count}
      <button onClick={() => dispatch({ type: 'increment' })}>+</button>
      <button onClick={() => dispatch({ type: 'decrement' })}>-</button>
    </div>
  );
}

title: Custom Hooks
content:
React Custom Hooks

Abstracting Logic

Custom Hooks are reusable stateful functions that start with "use". They allow you to extract complex component logic into reusable functions. For example, you could write a custom hook to fetch AI-generated recipes from a backend database and easily reuse it across multiple different app screens.

useFetch Custom Hook
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

// Defining the custom hook
function useFetch(url) {
  const [data, setData] = useState(null);
  
  useEffect(() => {
    fetch(url)
      .then((res) => res.json())
      .then((data) => setData(data));
  }, [url]);
  
  return data;
}

// Using it cleanly in a component
function App() {
  const data = useFetch('https://api.example.com/data');
  return <p>{data ? "Data loaded!" : "Loading..."}</p>;
}

title: Lifecycle
content:
Component Lifecycle

The Component Journey

Before Hooks existed, React relied heavily on Class components. Class components have specific lifecycle phases: Mounting, Updating, and Unmounting. While modern React primarily uses functional components with useEffect to handle these phases, you will still see these class methods in older codebases.



  • componentDidMount(): Runs once when the component is first added to the DOM.
  • componentDidUpdate(): Runs every time the component re-renders due to state or prop changes.
  • componentWillUnmount(): Runs right before the component is destroyed (perfect for cleanup).

title: Advanced Summary
content:
React Final Summary – Advanced 🚀

Mastering the Ecosystem 🏆

Congratulations! You have covered the advanced features of React. These powerful Hooks are the key to building highly scalable, modern applications.

  • Routing: Navigating between views seamlessly.
  • Global State: Avoiding prop-drilling by utilizing the Context API (useContext).
  • Advanced Hooks: Accessing the DOM with useRef and managing complex logic with useReducer.
  • Reusability: Abstracting complex fetching logic into custom Hooks.

With these skills, you are fully prepared to tackle complex frontend architectures!

title: ES6 in React
content:
React ES6 Features

Modern JavaScript

React heavily leverages modern JavaScript (ES6+) features to write cleaner, more expressive code. Understanding these JavaScript mechanics is crucial for mastering React.

  • Arrow Functions: const MyComponent = () => {} makes writing functional components and callbacks shorter.
  • Destructuring: const { name, age } = props; allows you to extract specific values from objects instantly.
  • Template Literals: Using backticks (` `) to inject variables directly into strings.
  • Modules: Using import and export to split code across multiple files.

title: Hooks Summary
content:
React Hooks Summary

The Core Arsenal

Hooks let you "hook into" React features like state and lifecycle methods directly from functional components. They completely replaced the need for older, clunkier Class components.

  • useState: Manage local component memory and trigger re-renders.
  • useEffect: Handle side effects like API fetching and DOM updates.
  • useContext: Access global state without drilling props through every layer.
  • useRef: Store persistent references to DOM elements without causing re-renders.
  • useReducer: Manage highly complex, multi-step state logic.

title: React Portals
content:
React Portals

Breaking Out of the Tree

Sometimes, CSS properties like overflow: hidden or z-index on a parent container can break visual components like Modals, Tooltips, or Dropdowns. React Portals fix this by letting you render a component completely outside its parent's DOM hierarchy, while still behaving like a child in the React Component Tree!



createPortal()
import { createPortal } from 'react-dom';

function Modal({ children }) {
  // This physically renders the HTML at the very end of the document body,
  // avoiding any CSS conflict with its actual parent component!
  return createPortal(
    <div className="modal-overlay">
      <div className="modal-content">{children}</div>
    </div>,
    document.body
  );
}

title: Best Practices
content:
React Best Practices

Writing Clean Code

As your app grows, maintaining a clean architecture is essential. Follow these golden rules:



  • Lift State Up: If two sibling components need the exact same data, move the useState hook up to their closest common parent and pass it down as props.
  • Top-Level Hooks: Never put React Hooks inside if statements or standard for loops. They must be at the top level of your component.
  • Single Responsibility: A component should ideally do one thing. If it gets too large, break it down into smaller sub-components.

title: Security Tips
content:
React Security Tips

Protecting Your App

React is inherently safe against many attacks (like automatically escaping text to prevent basic injections), but you must still be vigilant.

  • Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML: Never inject raw HTML directly into the DOM unless you have rigorously sanitized it first.
  • Validate User Input: Always validate forms to prevent malicious script injections (XSS).
  • Protect Routes: Ensure sensitive pages require authentication checks before rendering.

title: Projects
content:
React Final Projects Overview

Putting it into Practice

The best way to solidify your React skills is to build real-world applications. The logic you have learned here directly applies to modern stacks.

For example, in a comprehensive system like the MealMitra application, these fundamentals power everything. You use useEffect to fetch AI-generated recipes from the Python backend, store user data dynamically in Firebase, and manage complex UI states (like swapping between daily meal plans) using reusable React Components and Hooks.

  • To-Do List / Tracker: Master basic state and array mapping.
  • CRUD App (Like a Recipe Manager): Practice Create, Read, Update, and Delete operations.
  • API Data Fetching: Build a weather or news dashboard using useEffect.

title: Course Conclusion
content:
React Final Summary 🚀

You Are a React Developer! 🏆

You have successfully completed the comprehensive React curriculum. You now understand how to build fast, interactive, and highly scalable user interfaces.

  • Modern Syntax: Utilized ES6 destructuring, modules, and arrow functions.
  • State Management: Mastered the Hook ecosystem (useState, useEffect, useContext).
  • Advanced Rendering: Escaped the DOM tree safely using React Portals.
  • Architecture: Learned industry-standard best practices, state lifting, and security.

This is a massive milestone. Whether you apply this to React for the Web or React Native for mobile, you are ready to build incredible platforms!
